While Keir Starmer offers serious, level-headed leadership in the national interest, Nigel Farage and Kemi Badenoch have shown themselves to be unfit for office."

News.title: Joey Barton 'attack victim' pictured as Kevin Lynch after 'Huyton golf club assault'
News.content:
The alleged victim of a suspected assault with which Joey Barton has been charged in connection has been named.

Former footballer Barton, 43, and a second man, 50-year-old Gary O'Grady, have been charged with section 18 wounding with intent following an alleged serious assault by the Huyton and Prescot Golf Club in Liverpool on Sunday.

It has emerged that Lynch was the alleged victim of the assault. He is the head teacher at the NexGen Academy in Dovecot, an independent school set up to meet the needs of children with additional educational needs.

Image: Peter Byrne/PA Wire) Peter Byrne/PA Wire)

Lynch is also prominently known for his non-league footballing career, and most recently managed non-league side City of Liverpool, reports the Echo.



Before moving into management, the 50-year-old spent time in the non-league football pyramid with the likes of Droylsden, Marine, Vauxhall Motors and Southport, where he made 36 appearances, winning the Conference in 2005.

After hanging his boots up, Lynch moved into coaching, notably joining Chesterfield as their first-team and senior development coach.



It was in non-league across Merseyside and North Wales where Lynch remained after his playing career. He spent four years in charge of Marine, signing a two-year deal in 2010 while the club were in the seventh tier of English football.



Lynch left Marine in 2013 and took up the post at Colwyn Bay in 2015 before he was axed after a 3-0 defeat to his former club, bringing an end to his six-month stint with the Seagulls.



The 50-year-old made his return to the dugout to look after Marine’s reserve team in 2020 but was soon installed as Prescot Cables manager in 2021 with the club in a relegation battle. Lynch took up the post at Valerie Park following the sacking of Craig Davies.



He steered the club away from the danger zone in the Northern Premier League West, step eight of the football pyramid. But he left abruptly in 2023, resigning from the club due to personal reasons. He was replaced at Cables by then assistant manager Steven Daley.



In November 2024, he was installed as manager of non-league crisis club City of Liverpool, tasked with stemming the Purps’ downward spiral.



The Purps had been playoff finalists just six months earlier but the club endured difficulty, failing to secure their own ground and were forced to move to Widnes Vikings' DCBL Stadium. As a result of a difficult start, which saw the club stifled by their new ground move, lead to Paul McNally's dismissal and Lynch's appointment.

The move proved to be controversial among supporters following the appointment and after just 50 days in charge, scoring just eight goals and losing all eight matches, he left his position.

News.title: State Pensioners exempt from new savings rule if born before certain date
News.content:
State Pensioners exempt from new savings rule if born before certain date

Chancellor Rachel Reeves announced in the Budget that cash ISA limits will be cut from £20,000 to £12,000 for under-65s from April 2027, with state pensioners born before a certain date keeping the higher allowance

View Image You are exempt if born before a certain date (Image: alvaro gonzalez via Getty Images)

State pensioners will be exempt from forthcoming cash ISA rule alterations. The tax-free savings limit in cash Individual Savings Accounts (Isas) will be reduced from £20,000 to £12,000 annually for those under 65, as announced by Labour Party Chancellor Rachel Reeves in the Budget.

‌



The existing £20,000 yearly allowance can be utilised in a single account or distributed across various Isa products as desired.

‌



These accounts do not automatically close at the conclusion of the tax year. When the new tax year commences, you have the option to open a new Isa or, in certain instances, continue adding funds to your existing accounts.

‌



To open an Isa, you must be at least 18 years old. You also need to reside in the UK or be a member of the armed forces, or a so-called Crown servant working overseas.

State pensioners born before 1962 exempt from new cash ISA rule READ MORE :

In the Budget, the chancellor declared that the annual tax-free allowance for cash Isas will be lowered from £20,000 to £12,000 for individuals under 65, reports Birmingham Live.

This modification will be implemented in April 2027. This means anyone born before 1962 is exempt.

‌



The government's aim is to "ensure people's hard-earned savings are delivering the best returns and driving more investment into the UK economy".

BBC and ITV personality Martin Lewis commented: "There's logic in here based on the policy aims. Whilst I would've preferred a carrot, not stick approach – this isn't as bad as it could've been, £12,000 per year is still a reasonable whack for many people.

"The stated aim was not to raise revenue, but to encourage young people to invest rather than save – both for the economy, but also because on average it outperforms.

‌



"When I met the Chancellor on this a few weeks ago, I pointed out that a blanket cut to the limit would be perverse; to cut cash ISA limits for older people to encourage younger people to invest wouldn't work.

"So, the carve out for over-64s makes total sense and I'm pleased she listened.

"What needs to happen along with this is better investment education, easier access to guidance, and better investment incentives for young people."

News.title: 'I cooked sausages in frying pan, oven and air fryer and one was the obvious winner'
News.content:
'I cooked sausages in frying pan, oven and air fryer and one was the obvious winner'

A food writer tested three popular methods for cooking sausages to find which one produces the juiciest and most delicious results

View 4 Images There are various ways to cook sausages - but one produces a much better product (Image: Getty)

Sometimes a sausage sandwich can really hit the spot - but how is best to cook them?

‌



Sausages aren't amongst the meats I cook regularly, unlike streaky bacon, chicken breasts and salmon fillets. That said, I do enjoy them occasionally in a comforting casserole, paired with mash and gravy, in a straightforward traybake dish or as part of a sausage breakfast muffin.

‌



Many home cooks favour the frying pan for sausages, believing it delivers the crispiest outcome. Yet most chefs and butchers argue the oven is the superior method. Whilst the air fryer is my go-to when cooking sausages alone, I was keen to determine whether the frying pan or oven approach was best, so I tested all three techniques.

‌



American cooks full English breakfast for first time but makes 1 big mistake READ MORE :

Frying pan

View 4 Images The sausages fried in the pan turned out to be the worst (Image: Angela Patrone)

To fry the sausages, I began by setting the hob to medium-low heat and lightly coating the pan with olive oil spray. Once sufficiently heated, I arranged the sausages in the pan, ensuring they remained separate to prevent them sticking together.

I allowed the sausages to cook thoroughly on one side before turning them over. I resisted the temptation to constantly flip or pierce them, as this could disrupt the cooking process. This method took considerably longer than anticipated, as certain sections of the meat struggled to cook properly. I likely spent 25 to 30 minutes ensuring they were fully cooked through.

‌



Once finished, I was left with a grease-splattered cooker, a scorched pan and sausages that were unevenly cooked and blackened in numerous spots. The sausages boasted a satisfying crispness and were reasonably juicy, though the burnt flavour proved rather overwhelming.

Rating: 4/10.

Air fryer

View 4 Images Sausages cooked in the air fryer were far less greasy (Image: Angela Patrone)

‌



In contrast to the frying pan approach, the air fryer offers a hands-off, relatively speedy method that delivers sausages with a golden, crispy exterior and pleasantly moist interior.

For the air fryer technique, I gave the sausages a light misting of olive oil spray before setting them to cook for 23 minutes at 180 degrees. I deliberately avoided pricking them beforehand to keep the juices sealed in, and gave the basket a good shake midway through to prevent any sticking. When the timer went off, the sausages appeared far more uniformly cooked compared to the hob method.

Upon sampling, I discovered them to be succulent and considerably less oily, as the air fryer basket enables excess fat to drip away.

‌



Rating: 9/10

Oven

View 4 Images The oven cooked my sausages the best (Image: Angela Patrone)

I can't recall ever having cooked sausages in the oven previously, but having tried this technique, I'll definitely be doing so again. To prepare the sausages, I set my fan oven to 180 degrees and lined a baking tray with foil.

Article continues below

After arranging the sausages on the tray, I slid it into the oven and left them for just over half an hour. Midway through cooking, I flipped them to ensure they browned evenly. Pulling the sausages from the oven, it was immediately apparent they'd cooked uniformly - far better than those done in the air fryer.

Whilst this method takes the longest, it also produces the most succulent results. The inside was moist and flavourful, whilst the skin achieved a pleasant crispness without becoming overly chewy. What's more, this cooking technique means very little washing up afterwards. I simply binned the foil and gave the tray a swift rinse under the tap.

Rating: 10/10

News.title: British Airways announces major update on UK rescue flights from Middle East
News.content:
A major airline has given an update on its rescue flights with the Middle East conflict continuing to rage.

There have been thousands of Brits who were left stranded in the region following the outbreak of the conflict but now 11 days on, British Airways has announced it will suspend its daily rescue flights from Muscat “due to reduced demand”.

It added that the suspension of its flights to the rest of the region will continue until at least later this month because of “the continuing uncertainty of the situation in the Middle East and airspace instability”.

Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

Image: AFP via Getty Images) AFP via Getty Images)

The airline said: “We’ve cancelled all flights to and from Amman, Bahrain, Doha, Dubai and Tel Aviv until later this month and to and from Abu Dhabi until later this year. We’re keeping the situation under constant review and are in touch with our customers to offer them a range of options.

“We have limited seats remaining on our repatriation flights from Oman (Muscat) to London Heathrow on 11 and 12 March for customers with an existing booking. Following these dates, the flights will pause due to reduced demand but will be kept under continuous review.”

Yesterday the Foreign Office gave an update saying that 37,000 stranded people have now returned. It states: "More than 37,000 British nationals have returned to the UK since 1 March. Three charter flights have now left Oman and landed in the UK, the third flight landed in the UK earlier this morning.

"The UK government has chartered a flight out of Dubai which, subject to the situation on the ground, will leave later today. On Sunday, 30 flights departed from across the region carrying more than 7,000 British nationals - the highest number of Brits arriving in the UK in a single day since the beginning of the crisis.

Image: UGC/ANONYMOUS/AFP via Getty Imag) UGC/ANONYMOUS/AFP via Getty Imag)

"The UK government has chartered a flight out of Dubai which, subject to the situation on the ground, will leave later today.

"On Sunday, 30 flights departed from across the region carrying more than 7,000 British nationals - the highest number of Brits arriving in the UK in a single day since the beginning of the crisis. British national should continue to follow FCDO Travel Advice and local instructions, as the situation is volatile and could escalate."

Sir Keir Starmer has said that the UK is “working round the clock to keep British citizens safe” and acknowledged that Britons at home are “worried sick” for friends and family.

“Staff are on the ground to support those in need, Government-provided flights are helping people get home, and our tireless armed forces are flying across the region to defend our allies and our interests,” he said.

News.title: Parents told to 'stop using’ popular soap after emergency recall over fire risk
News.content:
Parents told to 'stop using’ popular soap after emergency recall over fire risk

The Office for Product Safety and Standards has issued a safety alert for Kids Stuff Crazy Foaming Soap, halting sales of certain batches due to burn risks from flammable gases including butane, isobutane and propane.

View 2 Images A safety alert has been for certain batches of a children's soap after it was found to contain flammable gases (Image: krisanapong detraphiphat via Getty Images)

The UK Government has issued a safety alert regarding a children's toiletry item, citing serious burn hazards connected to its usage. The Office for Product Safety and Standards (OPSS) updated their warning, yesterday confirming that sales of specific batches of the soap product have been suspended due to safety worries.

‌



The Kids Stuff Crazy Foaming Soap is thought to contain flammable gases, potentially causing burns if dispensed close to an open flame. The government's risk assessment stated: "The product contains butane, isobutane and propane, which are flammable gases. If the product is dispensed on to a flame or other ignition source, or comes into contact with a naked flame, the foam may ignite causing burns to the user."

‌



Stocked in various shades including pink, blue and purple, the Kids Stuff Crazy Foaming Soap comes in 200ml containers.

‌



Couple finds 8-word note on windscreen after parking outside their new house READ MORE :

Couple finds 8-word note on windscreen after parking outside their new house READ MORE :

View 2 Images Sales of the affected foaming soap batches have been suspended while further testing takes place (Image: OFSS)

In 2021, four-year-old Oscar Stubbs-Beddard suffered 17% burns when a foam soap sold by Home Bargains ignited from a candle. In 2021, a three-year-old was left in critical condition after a similar Paw Patrol mouldable foam exploded in the bath.

Oscar's dad Jon Beddard said the products should be recalled, telling the Telegraph: "He was just splashing around with the foam, it was on his face and chest, and a little bit flicked off and caught the candle down by the taps and the whole thing exploded. His skin was peeling off his arms and face like sheets of wallpaper. He was screaming and fitting and shaking, saying he was going to die. It was terrible.”

‌



Emma Hill, a serious injury specialist at Fieldfisher solicitors who acted for Oscar's family, warned that the bath foams do carry safety warnings, but parents can think that it relates to the aerosol can, rather than the flammable product itself.

She said: "They should be completely recalled. If you look at the similar incident with the Paw Patrol soap, there is clearly something highly flammable in these products. People just pick these items up off the shelf and think that it’s a sensory play thing so it will be fine.”

The implicated batch codes featured in the warning are:

‌



Pink Crazy Foaming Soap Batch: 26109E 3 Batch: 25947E 1

Batch: Batch: Blue Crazy Foaming Soap Batch: 26112E 5 Batch: 26112E 4 Batch: 25948E 3

Batch: Batch: Batch: Purple Crazy Foaming Soap Batch: 25651E 3

This also applies to any other aerosolised foaming soap products produced by Hothouse Beauty Ltd with the same flammability profile.

Whilst sales of these batches stay halted, the OPSS is encouraging shoppers to check whether they've bought any products displaying the batch numbers listed above. Anyone in possession of the affected item should verify the batch number and contact their retailer.

‌



BBC's Watchdog subjected the foam to laboratory analysis and found it "burned" for 15 seconds. Colin Sanders, a cosmetic scientist, voiced serious concern about the product's combustible components and demanded it be reformulated.

The company told BBC Watchdog that "the product carries clear and prominent warnings, including that it is an extremely flammable aerosol, must not be used near naked flames or ignition sources, and should only be used under adult supervision. It went on to say that it has also introduced additional on-pack safety measures, including cap stickers, to further reinforce safe use".

The firm stressed that product safety is regarded "extremely seriously" and confirmed "it is already progressing an updated formulation of Crazy Foaming Soap, which it expects to release later this year".

‌



The BBC reported: "Finally, Kids Stuff told us that bath time products should always be used in line with pack instructions and under adult supervision, and that children should never be left unattended in the bath or in environments where open flames or heated instruments are present."

A Kids Stuff spokesperson said: "We can confirm that the OPSS has issued a temporary Suspension Notice in respect of specific batches of our Crazy Foaming Soap range, whilst further investigations are carried out.

"We are engaging constructively with OPSS and are cooperating fully throughout this process.

Article continues below

"Product safety is central to everything we do. The product is labelled as an extremely flammable aerosol and carries clear warnings not to use near naked flames or ignition sources, and to use under adult supervision. We are also progressing an updated formulation of Crazy Foaming Soap, which we expect to release later this year."

In an update on March 9, officials said: "There are reasonable grounds to suspect that the products are not safe products and therefore may contravene Regulation 5 of the General Product Safety Regulations 2005 because the flammability characteristics of the products, when used under normal or reasonably foreseeable conditions, pose a safety risk, particularly to vulnerable persons including children.The producer is required to suspend the products from the market while further investigations are carried out."

News.title: 4 in 10 young people favour apprenticeships over a university degree
News.content:
4 in 10 young people favour apprenticeships over a university degree

Nearly half of young people believe that doing an apprenticeship is a better option than doing a degree amid rising student loan debt and AI growth.

View 3 Images Over a third of people have considered moving industries and taking up a trade instead (Image: Draper Tools)

Almost half of young people see becoming a tradesperson as a quicker way to earn £100k than going to university - with many having big plans to buy a house sooner. Young people are also worried about the impact of AI on their future careers, making traditional careers attractive to the next generation.

‌



A poll of 2,000 adults under 28 revealed just 19% think opting for university instead of an apprenticeship is a good idea, whilst 40% think doing an apprenticeship is a better option. More than a third of those polled are also keen to avoid mounting student debt.

‌



View 3 Images 6 in 10 people believe working in a trade is "cool" (Image: Draper Tools)

‌



Sarah Hartland from Draper Tools, which commissioned the research, said: “It’s fascinating to understand young people’s views on going into the trade.

“With such high university fees and interest rates, there is a growing number of young people looking at apprenticeships as the best way forward for them.

“While everyone should follow the career path that’s right for them, we know that there are critical skills shortages in many trades, so this current interest may turn out to be a real positive for the future of the UK’s construction sector.”

‌



Nearly half of men (46%) and over a quarter of women (28%) have considered moving industries to become a tradesperson.

Meanwhile, one in four would rather work in a hands-on role than be sat at a desk, research from OnePoll.com found. Thirty five per cent like to learn practical skills, 33% believe there’s a better work-life balance and 26% enjoy a sense of pride in creating or fixing something.

View 3 Images A quarter of people believe people working in the trades have more fun (Image: Draper Tools)

‌



Over 60% think working in a trade is a “cool job”, with 55% saying their perception of manual careers is more positive than five years ago. More than half of people polled believe working within one is better for personal mental health than working from a desk, with 26% also thinking tradespeople have more fun than office workers.

Beyond the financial benefits and fun, worries about AI were another factor for young people, with 33% saying recent advances in AI have made them consider a career in the trade. The most common trade jobs people would join if they were to move industries are painting and decorating (32%), electrical (20%) and landscaping (20%).

Fifty three per cent believe they will be able to buy a home more quickly if they become a tradie than going to university, with 32% keen to start earning soon. It also emerged just 14% of those polled own their home, with 39% living with their parents. Worryingly, 29% of university students would change their decision to join higher education if they could go back in time.

Article continues below

Sarah Hartland, of Draper Tools, added: "Young people have an increasingly positive perception of working in a trade. In an era of screen fatigue and rising burnout, it’s easy to understand the growing appeal of hands-on roles that potentially offer a different work-life balance.

“With the right tools and support, joining a trade can be a very fulfilling and rewarding career path, so we’re glad to see the awareness of this growing.”

News.title: Couple finds 8-word note on windscreen after parking outside their new house
News.content:
Couple finds 8-word note on windscreen after parking outside their new house

A couple who moved to a new house but only have on-street parking have been left confused after finding a note on their car when they parked it outside a neighbour's house

View 2 Images There's only on-street parking outside the couple's new house (stock image) (Image: Getty)

After moving into their brand new home - a couple were left horrified after they were given a strongly-worded letter from their new neighbour.

Relocating to a terrace home with on-street parking, they were left baffled after discovering the note on their car windscreen. Parking disputes frequently spark tension and conflicts between neighbours, whether it's unauthorised driveway use, vehicles blocking access, property damage or numerous other issues.

Drama surrounding parking spaces is commonplace, with residents often becoming fiercely "territorial" about their preferred parking locations.

Under UK law, homeowners possess no legal entitlement to park directly outside their own property on public roads. According to the RAC: "It is perfectly legal to park outside someone's house, unless the vehicle is blocking a driveway or a wheel is over a dropped kerb. Where no parking controls exist, drivers do not have an 'automatic spot' for parking outside their homes."

Despite this, many residents habitually park outside their properties - with some becoming particularly angry when fellow motorists occupy what they consider to be "their" designated space.

View 2 Images Parking can cause tensions between neighbours (Image: shomos uddin via Getty Images)

Posting on Reddit, one motorist expressed their astonishment at their neighbours' reaction after leaving their vehicle on the street outside the neighbours' property.

The driver captioned their thread "Why are people so territorial about on street parking?" and elaborated: "For context my partner has recently moved to a terraced street with only on street parking (only I have a car).

"I personally don't care about parking exactly outside the house and will just park wherever's easiest, I usually come down the back road and fill up the line from the front.

"Yesterday, as soon as we parked up outside a house across from ours, someone came outside and asked us to move the car as their partner was coming home. Fair enough, if I can just reverse back there and then so I did."

However, the next morning they discovered a note left on their motor - which had been positioned in the very spot the other neighbour had requested they relocate to. The message bluntly stated: "Do not park outside this house again. Thanks."

The Reddit poster went on: "I really can't be a**ed dealing with the pettiness that is likely to come but I also don't care as there's no restrictions and it's first come first served. I don't understand why some people care so much?".

Though they acknowledged: "In all fairness, people do leave a spot outside our house that I don't use (so I don't care when other people have used it) because my car can barely handle reversing on the 10+% gradient and I don't want to risk bumping anybody."

In the comments section, readers were quick to weigh in with their opinions. One individual remarked: "Same down my road. One neighbour has two cars but only one fits on the driveway so when their partner goes out, they'll move their car off the drive to reserve a space.

"Another neighbour puts his wheelie bins out on the road (illegal) just to save a space or he'll ask his mate to park in the middle of two spaces then ring him up when he's nearly home so he can move his car."

Article continues below

They added: "I just park where there's a gap and walk, I don't know why the neighbours find that so difficult." Someone else concurred: "My neighbour does the bin thing and I just move the bin. Right in front of her Ringo camera too, makes it even more awkward".

However, a third countered: "I'm no fan of weirdos that think they own the road but I do admit I'd roll my eyes if someone was routinely parking down the street in front of my house when the space in front of their own house was available. Just seems like you're causing a load of completely unnecessary inconvenience to everyone."

Whilst another queried: "Why are you parking outside other houses if there's a space outside your house that you don't use?".

News.title: Simpson and guide Poth win Paralympic silver for GB
News.content:
Neil Simpson and his guide Rob Poth won Great Britain's first medal of the Milan-Cortina Winter Paralympics with silver in the alpine combined.

It is a third Paralympic medal overall for the visually impaired skier, who had recorded two fourth-place finishes earlier in these Games.

In the alpine combined, skiers complete a run of both the super G and slalom, with their factored times combined to determine the final standings.

Simpson and Poth had sat in fourth after the morning's super G on the iconic Tofane piste, 0.57 seconds short of third place.

But in the afternoon's slalom, they recorded the fastest time to move up two places as Canadaâs Kalle Ericsson and his guide Sierra Smith fell out of contention.

Italy's Giacomo Bertagnolli and guide Andrea Ravelli defended their title from 2022, finishing 0.65 seconds ahead of Simpson and Poth overall, while Austria's Johannes Aigner and Nico Haberl won bronze.

"I'm really chuffed with the performance, some good skiing on the second run and we carried out the plan we had," said 23-year-old Simpson, who was born with nystagmus - a condition that causes involuntary eye movements.

"The visually impaired men's [category] is really strong, there are lots of really quick guys in there so I'm pleased to get on the podium."

News.content: Made of metal, it is likely to have been heated when the blaze was raging, then cooled and doused with water as the fire was overcome, so it will need to be inspected for damage.
